Q1: I kept a pretty steady routine throughout the course, honestly. I'd block out about four hours most days for studying, and I really relied on listening to lecture recordings and podcasts related to the material. Being an auditory learner, I'd often play videos or recorded lectures while doing other things, then sit down to take notes afterward. I tried to spread my 26 hours across the week evenly rather than cramming everything into weekends.
Q2: My pattern was fairly regular, though I'd be lying if I said deadlines didn't shake things up sometimes. I aimed for consistency because it helped me stay on top of my five online courses, but when assignments piled up, I'd shift my focus to whatever was most urgent. Generally though, I had set times in the morning and evening when I'd do my studying, and I stuck to that most weeks.
Q3: Usually I logged in to check if new lecture videos were posted or to see any announcements from instructors. I also went in to submit assignments since my completion rate was pretty high. Sometimes I'd log in just to see if there were discussion posts I should respond to, though I'll admit I wasn't the most active in forums. The habit of checking in daily kind of became automatic after a few weeks.
Q4: Yeah, after the first month or so, logging into the LMS just became part of my morning routine. I'd grab coffee, check my email, then hop onto the platform to see what was new. It happened naturally because I was taking five courses online, so there was always something to check. The consistent structure of the courses made it easy to build that habit.
Q5: The video lectures were absolutely the most valuable for me. I learn best by listening, so being able to watch and re-watch those recordings made a huge difference. I'd often watch them at 1.5x speed first, then go back and listen more carefully to parts I didn't fully catch. The discussion boards were also helpful when classmates explained concepts in different ways than the professor.
Q6: I tended to skip over the supplementary reading PDFs and some of the extra articles posted. It's not that they weren't good, but reading dense text just doesn't stick for me the way audio does. I'd skim them if they were required for an assignment, but otherwise I relied more heavily on the video content. Some interactive quizzes I also didn't bother with if they weren't graded.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to what I'd be assessed on or if it helped clarify something I was struggling with. I also valued anything that let me hear the material explained out loud. If something felt like busywork or wasn't clearly tied to learning outcomes, my motivation to engage dropped pretty quickly. I needed to see the purpose.
Q8: Optional activities definitely felt different—I approached them with less urgency and more skepticism about whether they'd actually help. Required activities got done no matter what because they affected my grade. With optional stuff, I'd ask myself if I really needed it or if my time was better spent elsewhere. Sometimes I'd try an optional activity and find it useful, but more often I skipped them.
Q9: Absolutely, I did a lot of studying that wouldn't show up in the LMS. I'd download lecture audio and listen to it on my phone while commuting or exercising. I also recorded myself summarizing key concepts and played those back. My extracurricular activities meant I wasn't always at my computer, so portable studying was essential for me.
Q10: There were plenty of times I was actively learning but the LMS wouldn't have known. Like when I'd listen to downloaded lectures during my commute or when I'd discuss course material with friends from my extracurriculars. I also spent time just thinking through problems in my head while doing other things. That kind of mental engagement doesn't leave a digital trace.
Q11: Yeah, this was common for me. I'd watch a video once on the platform, then download the audio version and listen to it multiple times offline. Same with lecture slides—I'd pull them up once, download them, and review them on my tablet later without being connected. The initial access was just the starting point for deeper study.
Q12: There were definitely harder moments, especially mid-semester when all five of my courses had major deadlines around the same time. My attendance dipped a bit during those periods, and I felt more anxious than usual. Even though my stress levels were generally manageable, those crunch times tested my resilience. I'd get through it, but engagement felt more like survival mode.
Q13: Fatigue was probably the biggest factor for me. Balancing five online courses plus extracurriculars meant I sometimes logged into the LMS but didn't have the mental energy to really engage. Competing responsibilities definitely affected my usage patterns—I'd prioritize whatever was most urgent rather than what might be most beneficial for deep learning. Confusion about course material sometimes made me avoid certain modules temporarily.
Q14: Honestly, yes. There were times I'd have a lecture video playing but my mind was elsewhere, thinking about other assignments or just zoning out. I'd technically be logged in and watching, but I wasn't absorbing anything meaningful. Those sessions felt unproductive even though they'd show up as engagement in the system. I learned to recognize when this was happening and take breaks instead.
Q15: I procrastinated on discussion posts more than anything else. Writing out my thoughts in forums felt less natural to me than listening and processing information aurally. I knew participating mattered, but I'd put it off until the last minute. Sometimes I'd avoid starting a challenging assignment because I wasn't sure where to begin, even when I knew the deadline was approaching.
Q16: Deadlines were huge motivators for me, maybe too much so. My LMS activity would spike in the days before something was due. I'd log in more frequently, watch videos I'd been putting off, and really focus my energy. It wasn't the healthiest pattern, but it worked for keeping my assignment completion rate high. Without deadlines, I probably would have been less consistent.
Q17: Feedback had a moderate effect on my engagement. When I got good scores on assignments, it boosted my confidence and made me more willing to engage with similar content. But when feedback was delayed or vague, I felt a bit lost about whether I was on the right track. I appreciated detailed comments because they helped me adjust my approach for future work.
Q18: The course structure definitely shaped how I participated. When modules were released weekly with clear expectations, I could plan my time effectively. I appreciated courses that front-loaded video content early in the week so I could watch and process before attempting assignments. Courses with scattered or unpredictable posting schedules made it harder to establish my routine.
Q19: Having video lectures as the primary content delivery method made engagement much easier for me as an auditory learner. What made it harder was when courses relied heavily on dense reading materials or had confusing navigation in the LMS. Also, when discussion requirements felt forced or artificial, I engaged less enthusiastically. Clear, audio-rich content was my sweet spot.
Q20: I think that could happen when students are genuinely trying to understand material but struggling to produce quality work. They might watch videos repeatedly, read discussions, and explore resources, but still find it hard to translate that into completed assignments. It could also be anxiety or perfectionism—being active but too paralyzed to submit anything. Sometimes the learning just doesn't translate to output.
Q21: That actually resonates with me somewhat. My exam score wasn't amazing, but my assignment scores were strong, and I passed the course. A lot of my learning happened offline through audio review and conversations with peers. Someone looking at just my login frequency might not see the full picture of how much time I actually spent with the material in other formats.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show patterns like when you're most active, how often you access materials, and whether you complete assigned tasks. But they miss the quality of that engagement—whether you're truly focused or just going through the motions. They also can't capture offline study, mental processing, or learning that happens through conversation. Logs show behavior, not understanding.
Q23: Being engaged means I'm actively thinking about the material, not just passively consuming it. It's when I find myself connecting concepts to other things I know, asking questions in my head, or wanting to discuss ideas with others. True engagement feels like curiosity and investment, not just checking boxes. It's the difference between hearing words and actually listening to understand them.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much time I spent listening to downloaded content away from my computer, or the conversations I had with classmates about course material during extracurricular activities. It also wouldn't capture my internal processing—the thinking I did while commuting or exercising. And it definitely wouldn't show the mindfulness practices I used to manage my anxiety, which actually helped me engage more effectively when I did sit down to study.
